heroOverview: Cryptosporidiosis is a diarrhoeal illness caused by apicomplexan protozoa of the genus Cryptosporidium — principally C. parvum and C. hominis — that infect the intestinal epithelium after ingestion of environmentally resistant oocysts. It is among the most common causes of waterborne diarrhoeal disease worldwide, responsible for approximately 8% of all diarrhoea cases in children under 5 in low-income countries and for major outbreaks in high-income countries when oocysts breach municipal water treatment. The 1993 Milwaukee outbreak, the largest recorded waterborne disease event in U.S. history, affected approximately 403,000 people from a single contaminated public water supply. In immunocompetent individuals, cryptosporidiosis causes a self-limiting 1-3 week bout of profuse watery diarrhoea; in people living with HIV/AIDS with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL, it can produce a relentless cholera-like illness leading to severe malnutrition, wasting, and death. The only licensed treatment — nitazoxanide — reduces illness duration in immunocompetent patients but offers limited benefit in advanced immunosuppression, where immune reconstitution with antiretroviral therapy remains the definitive intervention.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 4.8 million cases per year in children under 5 in low-income regions (Lancet 2017); approximately 748,000 cases annually in the United States (CDC estimate)
keyStats.demographics: Children under 5 bear the greatest burden globally; immunocompromised adults — particularly those with advanced HIV — face the highest risk of severe disease
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in children 6-24 months in low-income countries; all ages affected in outbreak settings in high-income countries
keyStats.globalCases: Cryptosporidium is the 2nd most common protozoal cause of diarrhoea globally after Giardia; found on every inhabited continent

definition: Cryptosporidiosis (ICD-10: A07.2) is an infection of the gastrointestinal (and occasionally respiratory and biliary) epithelium by protozoan parasites of the genus Cryptosporidium. The two species responsible for over 90% of human infections are C. hominis, which infects only humans (anthroponotic), and C. parvum, which infects cattle and other mammals as well as humans (zoonotic). The organism has a complex life cycle entirely within a single host: ingested sporulated oocysts (4-6 µm) excyst in the small intestine, releasing sporozoites that invade enterocytes in an intracellular but extracytoplasmic compartment just beneath the brush border. Asexual and sexual multiplication produces new oocysts that are shed in faeces in enormous numbers — up to 10^10 per gram of stool in immunocompromised patients — and remain infectious in the environment for months. The infective dose is very low: as few as 10 oocysts can cause infection. Pathogenesis involves villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and disruption of the intestinal brush border, leading to malabsorption, secretory diarrhoea, and, in severe cases, protein-energy malnutrition. In people with severely depleted CD4 T-lymphocytes, the infection extends to the biliary epithelium (sclerosing cholangitis-like syndrome) and rarely the respiratory tract. Cryptosporidiosis belongs to the clinical domain of infectious disease, gastroenterology, and HIV medicine, and is classified as a WHO neglected tropical disease and a U.S. CDC Category B bioterrorism agent due to its potential for waterborne outbreaks.

typesClassification.description: Self-limiting watery diarrhoea lasting 1-3 weeks; profuse in the first few days then gradually resolving. Dehydration is the main concern, particularly in children. Spontaneous recovery occurs without specific treatment in most cases.

typesClassification.type: Cryptosporidiosis in immunocompromised hosts (particularly advanced HIV)
typesClassification.description: Chronic, relentless, cholera-like diarrhoea producing 10-20 litres per day in severe cases. Associated with wasting, malnutrition, electrolyte imbalances, and high mortality without immune reconstitution. Biliary and pulmonary involvement can occur.

Community-wide epidemics from oocyst contamination of municipal water supplies or recreational water. Affects all age groups and immune states simultaneously; oocysts are resistant to standard chlorination.
Acquired from direct contact with infected calves or cattle, or from run-off contaminating water bodies; commonly affects farmers, veterinarians, and rural children.

primarySymptoms: Profuse watery diarrhoea — often 5-10 watery stools daily at peak illness, sometimes more in immunocompromised patients — caused by secretory mechanisms and villous blunting disrupting fluid absorption.
Abdominal cramping and pain, typically diffuse and colicky, coinciding with bouts of diarrhoea and reflecting intestinal dysmotility and mucosal inflammation.
Nausea and vomiting, more prominent in the first 2-3 days of illness and contributing to difficulty maintaining oral hydration.
Low-grade fever (typically below 38.5°C) during the acute phase, reflecting the host innate immune response to intestinal epithelial damage.
Anorexia and reduced food intake driven by nausea and abdominal discomfort, compounding dehydration risk, particularly dangerous in young children.
Dehydration — dry mouth, decreased urine output, dizziness on standing — the principal complication and cause of hospitalisation in children under 5.
Fatigue and generalised weakness persisting for 1-2 weeks beyond resolution of diarrhoea, even in otherwise healthy adults, reflecting the metabolic cost of acute intestinal illness.
Weight loss in proportion to illness duration; pronounced and progressive in immunocompromised patients where diarrhoea continues for weeks to months.
Right upper quadrant pain and jaundice in immunocompromised patients with biliary cryptosporidiosis, reflecting invasion of the bile ducts and sclerosing cholangitis-like changes.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset of watery stools — 3 or more loose stools in 24 hours — within 1-12 days of a swimming, drinking-water, or animal-contact exposure.
Progressive thirst and reduced urine output suggesting early dehydration, particularly in young children.
Nausea and abdominal gurgling preceding the onset of frank diarrhoea.
In a person living with HIV/AIDS, any new-onset diarrhoea warrants urgent stool testing given the risk of severe cryptosporidiosis.
emergencySigns: Signs of severe dehydration in a child under 5 — sunken fontanelle, no tears when crying, very reduced urine, loss of skin turgor — requiring emergency intravenous rehydration.
Profuse diarrhoea producing more than 1 litre per hour in an immunocompromised adult, indicating a medical emergency.
Jaundice with fever and right upper quadrant pain in an HIV-positive patient, suggesting biliary cryptosporidiosis and potential cholangitis.
Confusion, rapid pulse, low blood pressure, or cold extremities indicating hypovolaemic shock from dehydration.

causes.cause: Ingestion of oocysts in contaminated drinking water
causes.description: Oocysts are 4-6 µm in size, highly resistant to chlorine at standard water treatment concentrations, and survive for months in cold surface water. Filtration failures in municipal systems are responsible for major outbreaks; private wells and surface water sources pose ongoing risk.

causes.cause: Recreational water exposure (pools, water parks, lakes)
causes.description: Cryptosporidium is the leading cause of recreational water illness in the United States; a single diarrhoeal child excretes up to 10^10 oocysts, contaminating pools where the organism survives standard chlorine treatment. Swallowing even a small amount of contaminated water transmits infection.

causes.cause: Zoonotic transmission from cattle and calves
causes.description: Young calves are major reservoirs of C. parvum, shedding high oocyst loads in faeces. Farmers, veterinarians, and rural children acquire infection through direct calf contact or through contamination of water bodies from agricultural run-off — a dominant source of sporadic rural cases.

The low infective dose (as few as 10-30 oocysts) makes direct transmission highly efficient in households, childcare centres, and healthcare settings; thorough handwashing with soap after toileting is essential because oocysts are not killed by alcohol hand rub.

Oocysts deposited on fresh produce, unpasteurised fruit juices, raw shellfish from contaminated waters, or pre-cut salads can cause foodborne outbreaks; the organism is not inactivated by the typical acid pH of juices or by conventional cold-chain temperature.

In individuals with CD4 T-cell counts below 100 cells/µL (advanced HIV/AIDS) or severe combined immunodeficiency, a low-level infection that would self-limit in a healthy person is not cleared, resulting in uncontrolled intracellular replication and massive oocyst output sustaining a chronic debilitating illness.

riskFactors.description: Children aged 6-24 months carry the highest disease burden globally; immature mucosal immunity and poor hygiene practices combine with crowded environments to sustain high-rate transmission.

The most important risk factor for severe and chronic cryptosporidiosis; without effective antiretroviral therapy, cryptosporidiosis is a major AIDS-defining illness and a leading cause of AIDS-related death in sub-Saharan Africa.

riskFactors.factor: Swimming in public pools or recreational water
riskFactors.description: The risk is highest when young children are present and swimming while experiencing diarrhoea; not swallowing water and not entering pools while having diarrhoea are the key preventive behaviours.

riskFactors.factor: Drinking untreated or inadequately filtered water
riskFactors.description: Municipal water systems relying on chlorination alone without adequate filtration, private wells near agricultural land, and surface water from rivers and lakes are all potential sources; using only water treated by 1-micron absolute filtration or UV disinfection prevents oocyst ingestion.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational or recreational contact with cattle and calves
riskFactors.description: Farmers, veterinarians, agricultural students, and rural families in close contact with calves have significantly elevated cryptosporidiosis risk; C. parvum seroprevalence in UK and U.S. farmers is 2-3 times that of the general population.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to endemic regions with unsafe water
riskFactors.description: Travellers to regions without reliable water treatment infrastructure commonly experience Cryptosporidium as a cause of traveller's diarrhoea; bottled water and food hygiene reduce risk.

riskFactors.factor: Immunosuppression from organ transplantation or chemotherapy
riskFactors.description: Solid organ and haematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients and patients on immunosuppressive chemotherapy have elevated risk of severe cryptosporidiosis; prophylactic measures during periods of deep immunosuppression are appropriate.

riskFactors.factor: Attendance at childcare or exposure to nappied children
riskFactors.description: Childcare centres are high-risk transmission environments; Cryptosporidium is a leading cause of outbreaks in daycare settings in both high- and low-income countries.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5 in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and other low-income regions
People living with HIV/AIDS, particularly those with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL
Swimmers and recreational water users, particularly children in public pools
Farmers, veterinarians, and rural families in contact with calves and cattle
Travellers to regions with unreliable water treatment
Organ transplant recipients and patients on chemotherapy
Residents of outbreak zones served by insufficiently filtered municipal water
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cryptosporidiosis relies primarily on stool-based laboratory tests and should be considered in any patient with acute watery diarrhoea with a compatible exposure history. Traditional microscopy using modified acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen or Kinyoun) staining of concentrated stool smears detects the characteristic 4-6 µm oocysts with a sensitivity of approximately 70-80% from a single specimen; sensitivity improves to above 90% with three specimens examined over three days. Direct immunofluorescence assay (DFA) using monoclonal antibodies against the oocyst wall is more sensitive than acid-fast staining and is the preferred microscopy-based method in reference laboratories. Enzyme immunoassay (EIA) and rapid immunochromatographic lateral flow assays that detect Cryptosporidium antigen in stool have sensitivity around 85-99% and specificity above 99%, and are now widely used for rapid diagnosis. Molecular methods — multiplex PCR stool panels (e.g. FilmArray GI Panel) — provide the highest sensitivity and can simultaneously detect dozens of enteric pathogens including Cryptosporidium at species level, allowing speciation into C. hominis versus C. parvum, which has epidemiological and prognostic significance. For immunocompromised patients, multiplex PCR is the recommended initial test. In HIV-positive patients with diarrhoea, CD4 count should be assessed alongside stool testing. Biliary cryptosporidiosis in advanced HIV may be confirmed by bile duct imaging (MRCP, ERCP) or duodenal biopsy. Oocysts have not been reliably detected in blood; extraintestinal diagnosis requires biopsy of affected tissues.

diagnosticTests.test: Modified acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen or Kinyoun) stained stool smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Standard microscopy method for detecting Cryptosporidium oocysts in stool; the 4-6 µm pink-staining oocysts against a blue background are identifiable under 1000× oil immersion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Submit fresh or preserved stool in a specimen container; at least 3 samples collected on different days improve sensitivity. Laboratory results take 1-2 days. The concentration step (formalin-ethyl acetate sedimentation) precedes staining.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool antigen detection (EIA or lateral flow immunoassay)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid and sensitive detection of Cryptosporidium antigen in stool, suitable for clinical laboratories without specialist parasitology expertise; most tests can also detect Giardia simultaneously.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool specimen is mixed with reagents in the laboratory or on a test strip; EIA results take 2-4 hours, rapid tests 15-30 minutes. High sensitivity and specificity make these the front-line tests in many settings.

diagnosticTests.test: Multiplex stool PCR panel (e.g. FilmArray Gastrointestinal Panel)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Molecular detection of Cryptosporidium DNA with the highest sensitivity of any current method; simultaneously identifies common bacterial, viral, and protozoal enteric pathogens and can speciate Cryptosporidium.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fresh stool sample collected in a preservative tube is processed in the molecular laboratory; results are available in 1-4 hours for rapid panels or 1-2 days for batch PCR. Widely available in hospital laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard microscopy method using fluorescent antibodies against the oocyst wall; higher sensitivity than acid-fast staining and allows precise measurement of oocyst size for speciation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Concentrated stool smear viewed under fluorescence microscope; apple-green fluorescent oocysts are identifiable at 400-1000×. Available in reference and public health laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses immune status that determines severity of cryptosporidiosis and guides urgency of antiretroviral therapy initiation; CD4 below 100 cells/µL identifies those at risk of chronic life-threatening infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw from the arm sent to an immunology laboratory; results usually within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: MRCP or ERCP (in immunocompromised patients with biliary symptoms)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Magnetic resonance or endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography images the bile ducts for sclerosing cholangitis-like changes of biliary cryptosporidiosis; ERCP can obtain bile samples for PCR.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRCP is a non-invasive MRI scan taking 30-45 minutes. ERCP is an endoscopic procedure under sedation requiring bowel preparation and carries a small risk of pancreatitis.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment depends critically on the patient's immune status. In immunocompetent adults and children over 1 year old, nitazoxanide is the only FDA-approved treatment: 500 mg twice daily for 3 days in adults (200 mg twice daily for children aged 1-3; 100 mg twice daily for children under 1 year) reduces illness duration by approximately 1-2 days compared with placebo and decreases oocyst shedding. Adequate oral rehydration is the cornerstone of management in all patients, with intravenous rehydration for severe dehydration. Anti-motility agents such as loperamide can provide symptomatic relief from the frequency and volume of diarrhoea in adults.

In immunocompromised patients, nitazoxanide has modest efficacy at best, and antiretroviral therapy (ART) for HIV-positive individuals is the most important and effective intervention — immune reconstitution to CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL enables spontaneous clearance in most cases. For HIV-positive patients not yet on ART, cryptosporidiosis should prompt urgent ART initiation. For patients already on ART with treatment failure or for non-HIV immunosuppressed individuals, aggressive supportive care with intravenous fluids and electrolyte replacement is critical; total parenteral nutrition may be required in patients with severe malabsorption. Biliary cryptosporidiosis in HIV may respond partially to ART-induced immune reconstitution; ERCP with sphincterotomy provides symptomatic relief in biliary obstruction. No single antiparasitic drug has demonstrated consistent efficacy in advanced immunosuppression, though azithromycin and paromomycin are sometimes used in combination. Prevention through safe water, pool hygiene, and immune reconstitution in HIV is as important as treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Nitazoxanide
medicalTreatments.description: The only FDA-approved antiprotozoal treatment for cryptosporidiosis. Mechanism is inhibition of the pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase enzyme. Dose: 500 mg orally twice daily for 3 days in adults; dose-adjusted in children. Should be taken with food to improve absorption and reduce nausea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces diarrhoea duration by approximately 1.5-2 days and time to oocyst-negative stool in immunocompetent patients; efficacy is limited in severe immunosuppression — RCT data show approximately 56% parasitological cure in HIV-positive patients vs 7-8% with nitazoxanide in advanced AIDS.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) for HIV/AIDS-associated cryptosporidiosis
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for cryptosporidiosis in HIV-positive individuals. Starting or optimising ART to achieve viral suppression and immune reconstitution (CD4 above 100 cells/µL) enables the host to clear the parasite, which no antiprotozoal drug reliably achieves in severe immunosuppression alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immune reconstitution with ART reduces cryptosporidiosis-related mortality by greater than 90% in HIV-positive patients; approximately 70-80% of patients with CD4 recovery above 100 cells/µL achieve spontaneous parasitological cure within weeks to months.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (ORS)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO or UNICEF-formulated ORS replaces sodium, potassium, and glucose lost in diarrhoeal fluid. First-line for mild to moderate dehydration in all ages. Given as 50-100 mL/kg over 2-4 hours for mild-moderate dehydration in children; adults use 200-400 mL after each loose stool.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents dehydration-related mortality, which accounts for most childhood deaths from cryptosporidiosis; WHO estimates ORS prevents approximately 1-2 million childhood diarrhoea deaths per year globally across all aetiologies.

medicalTreatments.name: Paromomycin (with or without azithromycin)
medicalTreatments.description: A non-absorbable aminoglycoside sometimes used in combination with azithromycin 600 mg daily for refractory or AIDS-related cryptosporidiosis unresponsive to nitazoxanide. Limited by variable efficacy in advanced immunosuppression and GI side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit reported in case series; combination paromomycin 1 g twice daily plus azithromycin showed approximately 25-50% parasitological response in small studies of AIDS patients; not recommended as first-line.

medicalTreatments.name: Loperamide (symptom control)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-motility agent that reduces stool frequency and volume by slowing intestinal transit. Used for symptomatic relief in adults with intact immunity; generally avoided in severe dehydration and in young children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces stool frequency by approximately 50% per day in adults; does not clear the infection or shorten illness duration, but significantly improves comfort during self-limiting disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Boiled or UHT milk and lactose-reduced feeds in children
alternativeTreatments.description: Temporary lactose avoidance may reduce osmotic diarrhoea during acute illness because Cryptosporidium infection causes transient secondary lactase deficiency in young children; reintroduction of normal feeds is appropriate after recovery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Zinc supplementation in children
alternativeTreatments.description: WHO recommends zinc supplementation (10-20 mg daily for 10-14 days) in children under 5 with acute diarrhoea, including cryptosporidiosis; zinc reduces illness duration and diarrhoea severity and helps prevent subsequent diarrhoeal episodes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Strict pool hygiene (chlorine dioxide or UV treatment)
alternativeTreatments.description: Public health intervention that breaks the recreational water transmission cycle; UV light and ozone inactivate oocysts that survive chlorine, and CDC recommends hyperchlorination (to 20 ppm free chlorine for 8 hours) to decontaminate pools after a faecal accident.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist should be involved for any immunocompromised patient with cryptosporidiosis, for cases not resolving within 3 weeks in an otherwise healthy adult, and for HIV-positive patients in whom ART initiation or optimisation is needed. HIV specialists should be contacted urgently when advanced AIDS is suspected. A gastroenterologist is appropriate for biliary cryptosporidiosis requiring ERCP. In outbreak settings, public health authorities must be notified.
doctorSelectionGuide: For immunocompetent patients, a primary care physician experienced with infectious diarrhoea can diagnose and manage cryptosporidiosis. Immunocompromised patients benefit from joint care between an infectious disease specialist and their HIV or transplant physician. Look for a centre with access to multiplex stool PCR for rapid diagnosis. For biliary involvement, an ERCP-capable endoscopist is needed.
preventionStrategies: Do not swallow water when swimming in pools, lakes, or rivers — Cryptosporidium oocysts are chlorine-resistant and widely present.
Stay out of pools and recreational water for at least 2 weeks after stopping diarrhoea.
Drink water filtered through a 1-micron absolute filter or treated by UV disinfection; boiling also kills oocysts.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water after using the toilet, changing nappies, and handling animals — alcohol hand rub does not inactivate oocysts.
Wash and peel raw vegetables and fruits before eating; avoid raw shellfish in endemic areas.
For HIV-positive individuals with CD4 below 100 cells/µL, avoid recreational swimming and drink only water treated by filtration or UV.
Ensure early initiation and maintenance of effective ART for people living with HIV to prevent CD4 decline to vulnerable levels.
Report suspected outbreaks to public health authorities; early detection limits the number of people exposed.
lifestyleModifications: Wash hands with soap for at least 20 seconds after every toilet visit and before preparing food — the most effective single preventive measure.
Ensure children in childcare are excluded during diarrhoeal illness and for 48 hours after the last loose stool.
Keep immunocompromised family members away from farm animals, particularly calves.
Adopt a BRAT-like diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) during acute illness for tolerated, easy-to-absorb calories.
Use a filtered or UV-treated water pitcher or bottle consistently rather than trusting tap water in unknown locations.
Monitor hydration by urine colour — aim for pale yellow; dark amber indicates inadequate intake.

recommended: Oral rehydration solution to replace lost fluids and electrolytes throughout diarrhoeal illness
Bland, easily digestible foods — plain rice, boiled potatoes, bananas, plain crackers — during acute diarrhoea
Zinc-supplemented foods or supplements for children under 5 during and after diarrhoeal illness
Regular reintroduction of normal diet within 24-48 hours of symptom improvement to support nutritional recovery
Bottled (sealed) or boiled water in endemic areas and during travel
avoid: Untreated surface water or tap water in areas with known Cryptosporidium contamination
Dairy products temporarily in young children if lactose intolerance develops during acute illness
High-fibre or fatty foods that can exacerbate diarrhoea and cramping during acute illness
Unpasteurised juices, ciders, and raw shellfish in endemic settings

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is appropriate during the acute diarrhoeal phase, particularly when dehydration or systemic symptoms are present. Gradual return to normal physical activity is appropriate as symptoms resolve. Immunocompromised patients should avoid recreational swimming and contact with livestock throughout periods of low CD4 count to reduce reinfection risk. Otherwise, there are no permanent exercise restrictions after recovery.
dailyManagement: Track stool frequency and character — improvement should begin within 3-7 days in immunocompetent patients; plateau or worsening requires reassessment.
Maintain adequate fluid intake — measure output if severely ill; target pale urine output as a sign of adequate hydration.
Complete the full 3-day nitazoxanide course even if stools have improved.
For HIV-positive individuals, prioritise ART adherence and contact the HIV clinic if cryptosporidiosis develops.
Notify public health authorities if multiple household or community members develop simultaneous diarrhoea, suggesting a waterborne outbreak.
Report children to school or childcare only after 48 symptom-free hours to prevent spread.
prognosis: In immunocompetent individuals, cryptosporidiosis is a self-limiting illness: diarrhoea resolves within 1-3 weeks in virtually all cases, and long-term complications are rare. A period of post-infectious irritable bowel symptoms — bloating, irregular stool pattern — lasting weeks to months occurs in approximately 10-15% of adults after a cryptosporidiosis episode. In young children in low-income settings, repeated cryptosporidiosis episodes contribute to growth faltering, environmental enteric dysfunction, and cognitive impairment — effects that persist long after the acute infection clears. Malnutrition increases mortality significantly. In immunocompromised patients, prognosis is directly tied to immune status: individuals with HIV who achieve CD4 count recovery above 100 cells/µL on ART typically clear the infection and recover fully. Without immune reconstitution, chronic cryptosporidiosis is relentless, causing progressive wasting, malnutrition, and death in months. Biliary cryptosporidiosis may leave residual biliary strictures even after parasitological cure. With modern ART widely available, the incidence of fatal AIDS-related cryptosporidiosis has fallen dramatically in high-income countries, but it remains a major cause of mortality in sub-Saharan Africa where ART access is incomplete.
recoveryTimeline: In healthy adults, diarrhoea typically peaks in days 3-7 and resolves within 1-3 weeks. Energy and appetite take an additional 1-2 weeks to return fully. In children under 5, recovery is usually complete within 2-3 weeks though rehydration may be needed throughout. Immunocompromised patients on effective ART typically see improvement in diarrhoea volume and frequency within 4-8 weeks of ART initiation as CD4 counts begin to rise, with complete resolution by 3-6 months in responders. Post-treatment stool PCR or microscopy should be negative before discharge in immunocompromised patients.
complications: Severe dehydration and electrolyte imbalance — the most common cause of cryptosporidiosis-related death in children; requires intravenous rehydration.
Chronic diarrhoea and wasting — in immunocompromised patients, unrelenting fluid and protein losses lead to profound malnutrition and cachexia without immune reconstitution.
Biliary cryptosporidiosis — invasion of bile ducts causing a sclerosing cholangitis-like syndrome with jaundice, cholestasis, and risk of cholangiocarcinoma in long-standing cases.
Post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome — altered gut motility and visceral hypersensitivity persisting for months in approximately 10-15% of adults after acute infection.
Growth faltering and environmental enteric dysfunction — repeated early childhood cryptosporidiosis contributes to stunting and impaired cognitive development in low-income countries.
Respiratory cryptosporidiosis — rare extra-intestinal extension to the bronchial epithelium in severely immunocompromised patients, causing cough, wheeze, and pulmonary infiltrates.

supportResources.description: CDC guidance on Cryptosporidium risk in private water supplies, prevention measures, and treatment recommendations.

supportResources.name: CDC — Healthy Swimming: Cryptosporidium
supportResources.description: CDC information on Cryptosporidium as a recreational water illness pathogen, including prevention at pools and lakes.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cryptosporidium
supportResources.description: NHS plain-English overview of cryptosporidiosis symptoms, when to get medical help, and advice on prevention.

sources.title: Striepen B. Parasitic infection: Time to tackle cryptosporidiosis. Nature, 2013
Checkley W et al. A review of the global burden, novel diagnostics, therapeutics, and vaccine targets for Cryptosporidium. Lancet Infect Dis, 2015
Kotloff KL et al. Burden and aetiology of diarrhoeal disease in infants and young children in developing countries (GEMS). Lancet, 2013
Rossignol JF. Nitazoxanide: A first-in-class broad-spectrum antiviral agent. Antiviral Res, 2014
CDC — Cryptosporidiosis Surveillance — United States, 2011-2012. MMWR, 2015
NHS — Cryptosporidiosis: symptoms, causes and treatment

confusedWithConditions.name: Giardiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Giardia causes watery diarrhoea with characteristic sulphurous belching and prolonged malabsorption from small bowel colonisation; it is chlorine-sensitive (unlike Cryptosporidium), produces distinct flag-shaped trophozoites on microscopy, and responds to metronidazole rather than nitazoxanide.

confusedWithConditions.name: Isosporiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isosporiasis is caused by Cystoisospora belli, an apicomplexan protozoan also found in immunocompromised patients; it causes similar watery diarrhoea but the oocysts are larger (20-30 µm, elongated), it responds well to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and stool acid-fast staining shows clearly distinct morphology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cyclosporiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cyclospora cayetanensis oocysts are larger (8-10 µm) than Cryptosporidium, unsporulated when freshly passed (making them non-infective immediately), cause a more prolonged self-limiting illness with fatigue, and respond to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, unlike Cryptosporidium.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Entamoeba histolytica causes invasive colitis with bloody diarrhoea and has a 10-fold larger trophozoite (10-60 µm), extraintestinal spread to the liver, and responds to metronidazole plus a luminal agent — features absent in cryptosporidiosis.

relatedConditions.name: Isosporiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are apicomplexan protozoal infections causing watery diarrhoea disproportionately severe in immunocompromised patients, and both are diagnosed by acid-fast stained stool microscopy.

relatedConditions.name: Cyclosporiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another apicomplexan coccidian parasite causing watery diarrhoea transmitted via contaminated food and water; shares epidemiological features with cryptosporidiosis and co-exists in the same endemic settings.

relatedConditions.name: Giardiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common intestinal protozoal infection worldwide, sharing faeco-oral and waterborne transmission routes with Cryptosporidium; both cause malabsorptive diarrhoea and are detected by stool antigen testing.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Advanced HIV is the single most important factor for severe chronic cryptosporidiosis; cryptosporidiosis was one of the original AIDS-defining opportunistic infections and drove the development of effective ART regimens in part to address it.

faqs.question: What is cryptosporidiosis and how do you get it?
faqs.answer: Cryptosporidiosis is a diarrhoeal illness caused by the Cryptosporidium parasite, most commonly C. parvum and C. hominis. You get it by swallowing tiny oocysts in contaminated water (including swimming pools and rivers), on unwashed raw food, or through contact with infected animals or people. As few as 10 oocysts can cause infection.

faqs.question: How long does cryptosporidiosis last?
faqs.answer: In healthy people, cryptosporidiosis typically causes watery diarrhoea for 1 to 3 weeks before resolving on its own. Fatigue can persist for another week or two. In people with weakened immune systems — especially those with advanced HIV — the illness can become chronic and last for months without treatment of the underlying immune deficiency.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment for cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Nitazoxanide (Alinia) is the only FDA-approved treatment for cryptosporidiosis; it reduces illness duration by 1-2 days in healthy people. Staying well hydrated with oral rehydration solution is the cornerstone of care for all patients. In HIV-positive individuals, starting or optimising antiretroviral therapy is the most effective treatment, as immune reconstitution allows the body to clear the parasite.

faqs.question: Why are people with HIV at greater risk from cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: The immune system — specifically CD4 T-cells — normally limits Cryptosporidium replication. When CD4 counts fall below 100 cells per microlitre in advanced HIV/AIDS, the parasite multiplies unchecked, causing relentless cholera-like diarrhoea of 10 or more litres per day. This can be life-threatening without antiretroviral therapy to restore immunity.

faqs.question: Can you get cryptosporidiosis from a swimming pool?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cryptosporidium is the most common cause of recreational water illness in the United States. The oocysts survive standard chlorine disinfection in pools and lakes. A single infected child can shed billions of oocysts. Not swallowing pool water is the most effective way to prevent pool-acquired infection.

faqs.question: Does alcohol hand sanitiser kill Cryptosporidium?
faqs.answer: No. Alcohol-based hand sanitisers do not inactivate Cryptosporidium oocysts. Soap and water handwashing is required — the physical action of lathering and rinsing removes oocysts from the hands. This is especially important after using the toilet, changing nappies, and handling animals.

faqs.question: How is cryptosporidiosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by testing stool samples in the laboratory. Modern multiplex PCR panels that test for many pathogens simultaneously are the most sensitive method and are widely available. Stool antigen tests (EIA or lateral flow) are also accurate and faster. Older microscopy using modified acid-fast staining can identify the oocysts but is less sensitive than molecular testing.

faqs.question: Can cryptosporidiosis affect parts of the body other than the gut?
faqs.answer: In severely immunocompromised individuals, Cryptosporidium can infect the bile ducts, causing a sclerosing cholangitis-like syndrome with jaundice and right upper quadrant pain. Very rarely, the respiratory tract is affected in people with advanced AIDS. In healthy people, the infection is almost always confined to the intestine.

faqs.question: Is cryptosporidiosis the same as giardia?
faqs.answer: No. Both are intestinal protozoan infections transmitted in water and food, but they are different organisms. Cryptosporidium causes a self-limiting illness in healthy people but can be life-threatening in severe immunosuppression; it does not respond to metronidazole. Giardia causes a longer-lasting malabsorption illness and is treated with metronidazole or tinidazole.

faqs.question: Can Cryptosporidium be killed by boiling water?
faqs.answer: Yes. Boiling water for at least 1 minute (3 minutes at high altitude) destroys Cryptosporidium oocysts. Filtration with a 1-micron absolute filter also removes them. Standard chlorination at normal municipal water treatment levels does not reliably inactivate oocysts, which is why cryptosporidiosis outbreaks occur in treated public water supplies.

faqs.question: Should I stay off work with cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stay away from work or school while you have diarrhoea and for at least 48 hours after your last loose stool. If you work in food preparation, healthcare, or childcare, you must inform your employer and follow local public health guidance, which may require longer exclusion or a negative stool test before return.

faqs.question: Can animals give me cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Young calves are a major source of C. parvum infection. Farmers, veterinarians, and people who handle calves are at significantly elevated risk. Dogs and cats can also carry Cryptosporidium, though they are less important sources of human infection. Always wash hands after handling any animals, especially young livestock.

faqs.question: Is cryptosporidiosis a notifiable disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cryptosporidiosis is a notifiable disease in the UK, US, Australia, and most high-income countries. Laboratories and clinicians are required to report confirmed cases to public health authorities. This enables rapid investigation and control of outbreaks, particularly from shared water supplies.

faqs.question: Can cryptosporidiosis come back after it has resolved?
faqs.answer: In immunocompetent people, re-infection from re-exposure is possible but is rare without a new environmental source. True relapse from the original infection is uncommon after complete recovery. Immunocompromised individuals who have had cryptosporidiosis should maintain extra precautions around water and animal contact to prevent reinfection.

faqs.question: What should children eat during cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Offer small, frequent amounts of oral rehydration solution to replace lost fluids and salts. Once vomiting settles, reintroduce bland foods — rice, bananas, plain bread, boiled vegetables — gradually. WHO recommends zinc supplements (10-20 mg daily for 10-14 days) for children under 5 with any diarrhoeal illness. Avoid high-sugar drinks like fruit juices, which can worsen osmotic diarrhoea.

faqs.question: How do I know if I am dehydrated from cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Signs of dehydration include a dry mouth and throat, dark yellow urine or no urine for 8 hours, dizziness on standing, and feeling very tired. In infants: sunken eyes, no tears, dry nappy for 6 hours, and loss of skin turgor. Severe dehydration is a medical emergency requiring intravenous fluids.

faqs.question: How long can Cryptosporidium oocysts survive in the environment?
faqs.answer: Cryptosporidium oocysts are exceptionally hardy. In cool, moist conditions they survive for months — a key reason they persist in water supplies and contaminated soil. They are killed by boiling, UV radiation, ozone, and hydrogen peroxide at high concentrations. Sunlight and desiccation gradually reduce viability over days to weeks.

faqs.question: Are there any long-term effects after recovering from cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Most healthy people recover fully without lasting effects. Approximately 10-15% of adults experience post-infectious irritable bowel symptoms for months after recovery. Young children in low-income settings who have repeated episodes may suffer growth faltering and environmental enteric dysfunction affecting long-term physical and cognitive development. Biliary complications can persist in immunocompromised patients who have had biliary cryptosporidiosis.

faqs.question: Is cryptosporidiosis more common in summer?
faqs.answer: Yes. In high-income countries, cryptosporidiosis has a characteristic summer peak linked to increased recreational water use (pools, lakes) and agricultural activity. In low-income settings, peaks coincide with rainy seasons when surface water contamination from livestock increases. Childcare-related clusters occur year-round but peak when more children attend.

metaDescription: Cryptosporidiosis causes watery diarrhoea from a waterborne parasite. Learn symptoms, nitazoxanide treatment, HIV risk, and how to prevent crypto infection.
